comments.text: Hung Bui, Slava Naprienko, Alexandru Zaharescu, and I have uploaded our paper to the arXiv, wherein we unconditionally resolve Erdős problem #387. The paper is an updated version of an earlier paper by Hung, Alexandru, and I, which resolved Erdős problem #387 assuming the Generalized Riemann Hypothesis. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Hung Bui, Alexandru Zaharescu, and I have uploaded our paper to the arXiv. The answer to the question is negative, in general. The proof reduces to a "covering problem" and a "divisor problem," roughly along the lines suggested independently on this thread by Terence Tao. Somewhat surprisingly, we were only able to solve the covering problem under the assumption of GRH. The divisor problem relies on a variety of sieve theory and exponential sum arguments. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
We four (Hung, Slava, Alexandru, and I) are now working together to attempt to resolve #387 unconditionally.
With some back-and-forth GPT-5.5 Pro gave me an argument for an unconditional covering theorem without assuming GRH. Have a look at this note. This should finish the problem.
Yes, this looks quite similar to one of Slava's earlier notes. There is room to modify and refine the method.

Przemek, this is my construction, from my note and Lean formalization, public on GitHub since May 23, which your PDF cites as reference [5]. The fixed-B allocation (∏ g_i = k!, each g_i ≥ B, the BPZ §§6–10 estimates re-read as an interface) is exactly the argument I wrote and formalized, so your note above is a rewriting of it. https://github.com/slavanaprienko/erdos-387/blob/main/unconditional_bpz_patch.pdf It does complete the qualitative question. I've been working on it with Kyle Pratt, Hung Bui, and Alexandru Zaharescu, and we're preparing a combined paper that strengthens it to a quantitative unconditional form in the style of their recent preprint.

Using ChatGPT, I found a second reference where this problem is mentioned: page 351 of P. Erdős, R. L. Graham: On the prime factors of ${n\choose k}$, Fibonacci Quart. 14 (1976) no. 4, 348--352. There are various references establishing the conjecture for almost all $n$ for a fixed $k$, mentioned in the ChatGPT response. However, I actually think the answer to this question is negative if one fixes $k$ and chooses $n$ carefully, and matters seem to reduce to a covering congruence problem (of a type similar to that seen in the "large gaps between primes"/"Jacobsthal function" literature). Namely, it seems the problem morally reduces to: Conjecture 1: For any $m$, there exists $k > m$ such that one can cover $\{1,\dots,k\}$ by congruences $a_p \pmod{p}$ for $m \leq p \leq k$, such that $0 \leq a_p < p - (k \% p)$ for all $m \leq p < k$, where $k\% p \in \{0,\dots,p-1\}$ denotes the remainder of $k$ when dividing by $p$. Indeed, suppose that this conjecture held. Pick any $m$, let $k$ be as in the conjecture, and then pick a large $n$ with $n = k- a_p \pmod{p^j}$ whenever $p^j \leq k$, with the convention that $a_p=0$ for $p < m$. By Kummer's theorem, $\binom{n}{k}$ is not divisible by any prime $p$ less than or equal to $k$, so one can express $$ \binom{n}{k} = \prod_{j=0}^{k-1} \frac{n-j}{b_j}$$ where $b_j$ is the product of all the prime powers $p^i$, $p \leq k$ that divide $n-j$, which is some number that depends on $k$ and the $a_p$ but does not otherwise depend on $n$. Because of the covering hypothesis, each of the $b_j$ is divisible by at least one prime $m \leq p \leq k$, and so in particular $b_j \geq m$ and $\frac{n-j}{b_j} < \frac{n}{m}$. With further sieving, one can find $n$ in say $[x,2x]$ for a large $x$ such that the integers $(n-j)/b_j$ are in fact coprime to all primes less than say $x^{\varepsilon_k}$ for some $\varepsilon_k > 0$ depending only on $k$. So now the $(n-j)/b_j$, in addition to being bounded by $n/m$, are all almost primes with only $O_k(1)$ prime factors. With luck, a suitable counting argument should show that for most such $x$, the products of the factors of these almost primes all avoid the interval $(n/m,n]$, and so none of the factors of $\binom{n}{k}$ will lie in this interval; as $m$ is arbitrary, this would contradict the claim. So the task now splits into two subtasks: firstly, to prove the conjecture, and secondly, to work out the sieve theory step indicated above. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

I had an interesting conversation with ChatGPT about Conjecture 1 and beyond - link shared. Basically it showed how to arrive at slightly weakened Conjecture 1 using prime-gap machinery, using Maynard + yours results. Then it sketched what remains to be proved. I share the full conversation and an interesting part below. The conversation is in 3 messages: - first message is a summary and potential attack strategies - second message is on Weak Conjecture 1 - third message is on attacking the full Conjecture 1. From GPT-5.2: The Maynard / Ford--et al.\ prime-gap machinery can be viewed as giving \textbf{existence of coverings with forbidden residue sets} provided you can still choose residue classes with decent "hitting power". In your setting, the forbidden set for prime $p$ has size $k \bmod p$, so the allowed set has size $p - (k \bmod p)$. A natural weakened target that matches what large-gap constructions already tolerate is: Weakened Conjecture 1$\star$ (allow $o(k)$ survivors): For every $m$, there exists $k > m$ and choices $r_p \in [k \bmod p, \dots, p-1]$ for primes $m \le p \le k$ such that \textbf{all but $o(k)$} integers $1 \le j \le k$ satisfy $j \equiv r_p \pmod p$ for some such prime $p$. Why this is the right weakening: Maynard explicitly notes that in the prime-gap construction it is ''permissible'' to leave a controlled number of survivors and then eliminate them later. In your binomial application, leaving $o(k)$ ''bad'' indices $j$ (where you don't force a prime $\ge m$ into $b_j$) might still be salvageable: you'd get a product of $(k - o(k))$ factors $\le n/m$ plus a small exceptional set of larger factors, and then you can try to keep divisors out of $(n/m, n]$ by controlling how many ''large'' factors exist. What you would need to import from prime-gap land? You'd want a ''covering lemma with constraints'' of this kind: Given a set $A \subseteq \{1, \dots, k\}$ of remaining uncovered points, and a prime $p$, among the \textbf{allowed} residue classes $r \in [k \bmod p, \dots, p-1]$, there exists one that hits at least $\approx |A| / (p - (k \bmod p))$ points of $A$. Then a greedy sieve over primes $p \in [m, k]$ would shrink the uncovered set by a multiplicative factor roughly\[ \prod_{m \le p \le k} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{p - (k \bmod p)} \right), \]which heuristically behaves like a power of $\log m / \log k$. This is exactly the kind of multiplicative shrinkage analysis that underlies ''greedy sieve'' discussions in the prime-gap literature and expositions.

I proved Conjecture 1 (as written) and formalized it in Lean 4. The proof also gives additional structural properties of the cover that may be useful for the sieve step: the nonzero residues are very sparse, mostly \(0\), with the nonzero ones living among primes \(p \equiv 1 \pmod{q}\) in \((k/2, k]\) (where \(q\) is the least prime \(\ge m\)). Writeup with the lemma statement and Lean formalization: - https://github.com/slavanaprienko/erdos-387/blob/main/cover_lemma.pdf - https://github.com/slavanaprienko/erdos-387 I think a bit more may be needed than Conjecture 1 alone to get to the Kummer's step. Conjecture 1 gives a cover \(\pmod{p}\), but applying Kummer's theorem to \(\binom{n}{k}\) at all prime powers \(p^j \le k\) seems to require additional compatibility at the higher digits of the base-\(p\) expansion. A residue \(a_p\) allowed \(\pmod{p}\) can lift to \(\pmod{p^2}\) in a way that puts the wrong count of multiples of \(p^2\) inside \(\{1,\dots,k\}\). Is the conjecture as stated what you intended, or should the residues come from the upper tail $(a_p \in {k \bmod p,\dots,p-1})$, which is what Lucas seems to need? UPD: Actually, I think I figured out how to change the cover lemma to get to the Kummer's step. I'll post an update soon.

The stronger conjecture is false (in particularly $c\geq 1/2$ is not good) if the prime tuples (Dickson's) conjecture on linear forms is true. Details: let $n=360360m+99215$ and $k=15$ then easily: $\binom{n}{k}=(72072m+19843)(180180m+49607)(120120m+33071)(90090m+24803)\\ (51480m+14173)(12012m+3307)(32760m+9019)(45045m+12401)(40040m+11023)\\ (180180m+49603)(72072m+19841)(4290m+1181)(27720m+7631)(180180m+49601)\\ (120120m+33067)$ Easily these $15$ linear forms are admissible, so if the prime tuples conjecture on linear forms is true, then for infinitely many $m$ integers $\binom{360360m+99215}{15}$ is the product of $15$ prime factors, the smallest is $\frac{n-11}{84}$, the largest is $\frac{n-1}{2}$, giving that these factors are smaller than $\frac{n}{2}$, and the product of at least two of them is way larger than $n$, hence it has no divisor in the $(\frac{n}{2},n]$ interval. To see this in practice for one $m$ value, the smallest solution is $m=494660932170922548$, found by wheel sieve+prp test+true primality tests in some ten days of computation. That is $\binom{n}{k}$ has no divisor in the $(\frac{n}{2},n]$ interval for $n=178256013517113649496495,k=15$. Ofcourse we'd need this for infinitely many $m$ to refute $c=\frac{1}{2}$. Btw $k=15$ is the smallest $k$ value for that this type of construction works if we need $k$ prime factors for some fixed $c<1$. If Dickson's conjecture is true, then there are $a(k)\geq 0,b(k)>0$ integers for that there is a divisor in the $[f(k),n]=[\frac{n-a(k)}{b(k)},n]$ interval for large $n$, and this is sharp, so it is possible that $(\frac{n-a(k)}{b(k)},n]$ interval already contains no divisor. (and ofcourse this $[f(k),n]$ interval also contains a divisor if some numbers is not prime). Furthermore: there is a divisor in the $[\frac{n-k+1}{\sqrt[k]{k!}},n]$ interval, and in lots case this is slightly better than the above $[\frac{n}{k},n]$ interval, since for "large" fixed $k$ as $n$ goes to infinity we have $\frac{n-k+1}{\sqrt[k]{k!}}$ is roughly $\frac{en}{k}$. To see this better bound, observe that: $\binom{n}{k}=\prod_{i=0}^{k-1}\frac{n-i}{d_{i}}$, where $\prod_{i=0}^{k-1}d_{i}=k!$. From this $\min(d_{i})\leq \sqrt[k]{k!}$ (this is also an Erdos problem, and it is close to optimal), giving that for some $i$ index: $n\geq \frac{n-i}{d_{i}}\geq \frac{n-k+1}{\sqrt[k]{k!}}$.
Hung Bui, Alexandru Zaharescu, and I have been working on this problem for some time, and are in the process of writing up our results in a paper.

Thanks for mentioning this. Feel free to click on the "Currently working on the problem" link above, which is intended to help coordinate these sorts of things! (I had quietly referenced this collaboration at https://github.com/teorth/erdosproblems/issues/151 , but perhaps too quietly.) I'll take this opportunity to share a relevant reference (which I got from Kyle a few months ago): this problem is also posed in pages 315-316 of P. Erdős: On prime factors of binomial coefficients, II., Mat. Lapok 30 (1978/82) no. 4, 307--316 (in Hungarian, but translation is trivial these days). Interestingly, by that time Erdos doubted that this conjecture is true. ("Most úgy gondolom, hogy e sejtés nem igaz.") Perhaps soon his doubts will be proven correct? (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
Ah, for some reason I thought I needed more posts before I could click the "Currently working on the problem" link. Thanks.
